title: Septic Shock
oneLiner: Life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated infection response; requires immediate antibiotics, fluids, and vasopressors to reduce mortality.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cardiogenic shock** – single finding: elevated JVP + clear lung fields + no fever + ECG changes (e.g., ST elevation) → bedside echo shows poor LV function  
- **Massive PE** – single finding: sudden onset pleuritic chest pain + hypoxia + right heart strain on ECG (S1Q3T3) + no infection source  
- **Anaphylaxis** – single finding: urticaria/wheal + wheeze + onset within minutes of a trigger (drug/injection/food)
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** fever (>38.5°C) or hypothermia (<36°C), rigors, tachycardia, tachypnoea, altered consciousness (confusion/agitation/lethargy), oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h)  
- **Key exam findings:** hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg), cool peripheries, delayed cap refill (>3 s), mottled skin, warm extremities in early distributive shock, respiratory distress, focal signs of infection (e.g., crackles, suprapubic tenderness, cellulitis, abdominal tenderness)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Septic shock (infection source + lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluids)  
- **Less likely:**  
  - Cardiogenic shock (elevated JVP, clear chest, no fever, ECG/echo abnormal)  
  - Hypovolaemic shock (clear fluid loss, flat neck veins, no fever)  
  - Obstructive shock (tamponade – muffled heart sounds + Kussmaul sign; massive PE – raised JVP + RV strain)  
  - Anaphylaxis (skin signs, wheeze, rapid onset)  
- **Splitter:** *Lactate >2 mmol/L + documented infection source* points to septic shock; *absence of infection + history of chest pain/dyspnoea + elevated BNP* points to cardiogenic shock
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - **NEWS2** every 15-30 min initially, lactate (point-of-care), urine output via catheter, ECG (rule out arrhythmia/MI), ABG (lactate, pH, pO2, pCO2)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation screen, CRP, procalcitonin  
  - Blood cultures (×2 sets aerobe + anaerobe) – **do not delay antibiotics beyond 1 hour**  
  - Lactate (arterial or venous), troponin, BNP if cardiogenic concern  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: CXR (pneumonia/effusion), USS (FAST – free fluid, abscess; renal bladder – pyelonephritis)  
  - Second-line: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if source uncertain (e.g., intra-abdominal collection, necrotising fasciitis)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Echocardiogram (if suspected LV dysfunction or persistent hypotension)  
  - Urine antigen (Legionella/pneumococcus) if community-acquired pneumonia  
  - Procalcitonin trend (to guide antibiotic de-escalation)  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - Sepsis-3: qSOFA ≥2 (RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered mentation) + lactate >2 mmol/L → *septic shock* if vasopressors required after 30 mL/kg fluids  
  - NICE: red flag criteria – NEWS ≥5, lactate >2, SBP <90, RR >25, breakthrough seizure, chemotherapy/immunosuppression  
- **Never-miss test:** *Blood cultures* (×2 sets before antibiotics – but never delay antibiotics >1 hour to obtain them)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **ABCDE** approach – give high-flow O₂ (target SpO₂ ≥94%), gain IV/IO access, start 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 15-30 min (if hypotension or lactate >4)  
  - **Antibiotics** broad spectrum within 1 hour (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV, plus gentamicin if local policy; cover MRSA with vancomycin if risk factors)  
  - **Vasopressor** if MAP <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg fluid – start noradrenaline IV infusion 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min (peripheral via large vein if central access not yet available)  
  - **Source control** – remove central lines, drain abscess, request urgent surgical review if source found  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Reassess fluid status hourly (haemodynamics, lactate clearance, urine output)  
  - Titrate noradrenaline to MAP ≥65 mmHg; add vasopressin or dobutamine if refractory  
  - Continue organ support: monitor renal function, consider CVVH if AKI with oliguria  
  - De-escalate antibiotics once cultures returned (e.g., narrow to targeted agent)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Transition to oral antibiotics when stable and source controlled  
  - Post-intensive care follow-up (weakness, cognition, rehabilitation)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** Balanced crystalloid (e.g., Plasmalyte) or 0.9% saline – continue at 100 mL/h initially, adjust based on dynamic assessment (fluid challenge)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) – hold if platelets <50 or coagulopathy  
  - **NBM** only if suspicion of ileus/obstruction or planned procedure  
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 30 min until stable, then 1-2 hourly; urine output hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line antibiotics:**  
  - Community-acquired sepsis: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h – [check local policy] for alternative (ceftriaxone+metronidazole)  
  - Hospital-acquired: tazocin as above ± gentamicin (once daily dosing – [check local policy])  
  - MRSA risk: vancomycin (loading 25-30 mg/kg then 15-20 mg/kg IV q12h, monitor trough)  
- **Vasopressors:**  
  - Noradrenaline: IV infusion 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP ≥65  
  - Alternative: vasopressin 0.01-0.04 U/min → dobutamine 5-20 mcg/kg/min (if LV dysfunction)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Beta-blockers reduce compensatory tachy – avoid starting; check K+ before giving insulin if DKA  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Piperacillin-tazobactam: reduce frequency if CrCl <20 mL/min – [check local policy]  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Not starting vasopressor early** – under‐resuscitation kills; second error: **giving excessive fluid** without reassessment (leads to pulmonary oedema)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** ARDS, AKI, DIC, multi-organ failure, metabolic acidosis, refractory shock, nosocomial superinfection  
- **Chronic:** Post-intensive care syndrome (muscle weakness, cognitive impairment, PTSD), chronic renal impairment
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality 20-50% for septic shock; each hour delay to antibiotics increases mortality by 7-10%. Early source control and lactate clearance >20% at 6h improve outcomes.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do:** Measure lactate at baseline and q2h for first 6h – falling lactate indicates good response  
- **Do:** Start noradrenaline via peripheral large vein (e.g., antecubital) if central access delayed – safe short-term  
- **Do:** Rapidly obtain source control (remove lines, drain collections, request surgical review) – this is lifesaving
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t:** Give 30 mL/kg fluids if patient in pulmonary oedema without assessing – consider inotropes first  
- **Don’t:** Delay antibiotics beyond 1 hour to obtain cultures – obtain cultures immediately but start ABx  
- **Don’t:** Forget to check adrenal insufficiency (e.g., random cortisol <200 nmol/L) if refractory hypotension despite noradrenaline
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Fever recurs, confusion/lethargy, worsening breathlessness, reduced urine output (<300 mL over 6 h), or hypotension on home BP check  
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48 hours; consider post-sepsis clinic for rehabilitation
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥7  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L not falling after 2 hours  
  - Requiring noradrenaline >0.2 mcg/kg/min  
  - GCS <13 or new confusion  
  - Metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2, base excess <-6)  
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 hours)  
- **Specific thresholds:** MAP <65 despite two vasopressors; failure to lactate clear by >20% in 6h

rawMarkdown:
## Septic Shock  
**One-liner:** Life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated infection response; requires immediate antibiotics, fluids, and vasopressors to reduce mortality.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?  
- **Cardiogenic shock** – single finding: elevated JVP + clear lung fields + no fever + ECG changes (e.g., ST elevation) → bedside echo shows poor LV function  
- **Massive PE** – single finding: sudden onset pleuritic chest pain + hypoxia + right heart strain on ECG (S1Q3T3) + no infection source  
- **Anaphylaxis** – single finding: urticaria/wheal + wheeze + onset within minutes of a trigger (drug/injection/food)  

### Presentation  
- **Cardinal symptoms:** fever (>38.5°C) or hypothermia (<36°C), rigors, tachycardia, tachypnoea, altered consciousness (confusion/agitation/lethargy), oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h)  
- **Key exam findings:** hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg), cool peripheries, delayed cap refill (>3 s), mottled skin, warm extremities in early distributive shock, respiratory distress, focal signs of infection (e.g., crackles, suprapubic tenderness, cellulitis, abdominal tenderness)  

### Differential Diagnosis  
- **Most likely:** Septic shock (infection source + lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluids)  
- **Less likely:**  
  - Cardiogenic shock (elevated JVP, clear chest, no fever, ECG/echo abnormal)  
  - Hypovolaemic shock (clear fluid loss, flat neck veins, no fever)  
  - Obstructive shock (tamponade – muffled heart sounds + Kussmaul sign; massive PE – raised JVP + RV strain)  
  - Anaphylaxis (skin signs, wheeze, rapid onset)  
- **Splitter:** *Lactate >2 mmol/L + documented infection source* points to septic shock; *absence of infection + history of chest pain/dyspnoea + elevated BNP* points to cardiogenic shock  

### Investigations  
- **Bedside:**  
  - **NEWS2** every 15-30 min initially, lactate (point-of-care), urine output via catheter, ECG (rule out arrhythmia/MI), ABG (lactate, pH, pO2, pCO2)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation screen, CRP, procalcitonin  
  - Blood cultures (×2 sets aerobe + anaerobe) – **do not delay antibiotics beyond 1 hour**  
  - Lactate (arterial or venous), troponin, BNP if cardiogenic concern  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: CXR (pneumonia/effusion), USS (FAST – free fluid, abscess; renal bladder – pyelonephritis)  
  - Second-line: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if source uncertain (e.g., intra-abdominal collection, necrotising fasciitis)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Echocardiogram (if suspected LV dysfunction or persistent hypotension)  
  - Urine antigen (Legionella/pneumococcus) if community-acquired pneumonia  
  - Procalcitonin trend (to guide antibiotic de-escalation)  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - Sepsis-3: qSOFA ≥2 (RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered mentation) + lactate >2 mmol/L → *septic shock* if vasopressors required after 30 mL/kg fluids  
  - NICE: red flag criteria – NEWS ≥5, lactate >2, SBP <90, RR >25, breakthrough seizure, chemotherapy/immunosuppression  
- **Never-miss test:** *Blood cultures* (×2 sets before antibiotics – but never delay antibiotics >1 hour to obtain them)  

### Management  
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **ABCDE** approach – give high-flow O₂ (target SpO₂ ≥94%), gain IV/IO access, start 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 15-30 min (if hypotension or lactate >4)  
  - **Antibiotics** broad spectrum within 1 hour (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV, plus gentamicin if local policy; cover MRSA with vancomycin if risk factors)  
  - **Vasopressor** if MAP <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg fluid – start noradrenaline IV infusion 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min (peripheral via large vein if central access not yet available)  
  - **Source control** – remove central lines, drain abscess, request urgent surgical review if source found  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Reassess fluid status hourly (haemodynamics, lactate clearance, urine output)  
  - Titrate noradrenaline to MAP ≥65 mmHg; add vasopressin or dobutamine if refractory  
  - Continue organ support: monitor renal function, consider CVVH if AKI with oliguria  
  - De-escalate antibiotics once cultures returned (e.g., narrow to targeted agent)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Transition to oral antibiotics when stable and source controlled  
  - Post-intensive care follow-up (weakness, cognition, rehabilitation)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** Balanced crystalloid (e.g., Plasmalyte) or 0.9% saline – continue at 100 mL/h initially, adjust based on dynamic assessment (fluid challenge)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) – hold if platelets <50 or coagulopathy  
  - **NBM** only if suspicion of ileus/obstruction or planned procedure  
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 30 min until stable, then 1-2 hourly; urine output hourly  

### Drugs & Doses  
- **First-line antibiotics:**  
  - Community-acquired sepsis: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h – [check local policy] for alternative (ceftriaxone+metronidazole)  
  - Hospital-acquired: tazocin as above ± gentamicin (once daily dosing – [check local policy])  
  - MRSA risk: vancomycin (loading 25-30 mg/kg then 15-20 mg/kg IV q12h, monitor trough)  
- **Vasopressors:**  
  - Noradrenaline: IV infusion 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP ≥65  
  - Alternative: vasopressin 0.01-0.04 U/min → dobutamine 5-20 mcg/kg/min (if LV dysfunction)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Beta-blockers reduce compensatory tachy – avoid starting; check K+ before giving insulin if DKA  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Piperacillin-tazobactam: reduce frequency if CrCl <20 mL/min – [check local policy]  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Not starting vasopressor early** – under‐resuscitation kills; second error: **giving excessive fluid** without reassessment (leads to pulmonary oedema)  

### Complications  
- **Acute:** ARDS, AKI, DIC, multi-organ failure, metabolic acidosis, refractory shock, nosocomial superinfection  
- **Chronic:** Post-intensive care syndrome (muscle weakness, cognitive impairment, PTSD), chronic renal impairment  

### Prognosis  
- Mortality 20-50% for septic shock; each hour delay to antibiotics increases mortality by 7-10%. Early source control and lactate clearance >20% at 6h improve outcomes.  

### Pearls (positive actions)  
- **Do:** Measure lactate at baseline and q2h for first 6h – falling lactate indicates good response  
- **Do:** Start noradrenaline via peripheral large vein (e.g., antecubital) if central access delayed – safe short-term  
- **Do:** Rapidly obtain source control (remove lines, drain collections, request surgical review) – this is lifesaving  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)  
- **Don’t:** Give 30 mL/kg fluids if patient in pulmonary oedema without assessing – consider inotropes first  
- **Don’t:** Delay antibiotics beyond 1 hour to obtain cultures – obtain cultures immediately but start ABx  
- **Don’t:** Forget to check adrenal insufficiency (e.g., random cortisol <200 nmol/L) if refractory hypotension despite noradrenaline  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)  
- **Return if:** Fever recurs, confusion/lethargy, worsening breathlessness, reduced urine output (<300 mL over 6 h), or hypotension on home BP check  
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48 hours; consider post-sepsis clinic for rehabilitation  

### Escalation Criteria  
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥7  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L not falling after 2 hours  
  - Requiring noradrenaline >0.2 mcg/kg/min  
  - GCS <13 or new confusion  
  - Metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2, base excess <-6)  
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 hours)  
- **Specific thresholds:** MAP <65 despite two vasopressors; failure to lactate clear by >20% in 6h
